skill.name: lark-attendance
skill.description: Guides Lark CLI use for querying personal Lark attendance punch records.
skill.summary: Query personal attendance records in Lark through lark-cli.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: The static Ruby backtick findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code and fenced examples, not Ruby execution. The skill does require external lark-cli commands and contains a parent-directory reference to ../lark-shared/SKILL.md, so publication should include a warning and package-boundary review. No evidence found for the weak cryptographic algorithm alert at SKILL.md line 4.

medium_findings.title: Parent Directory Skill Dependency
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md instructs the model to read ../lark-shared/SKILL.md before use. This crosses the skill package boundary and can hide important behavior outside the audited file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The parent-directory reference is explicit and located in a mandatory instruction. It may be a legitimate shared documentation pattern, but it creates package-boundary risk.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution Required
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to run lark-cli schema and lark-cli attendance commands. This is expected for a Lark CLI skill, but it depends on local binary behavior, authentication state, and command arguments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are clear and directly tied to the skill workflow. The risk is moderate because the commands are documented tool usage, not evidence of malicious execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive Markdown Backtick Matches
low_findings.description: Most static external command alerts match Markdown inline code, JSON examples, or fenced code text. These are documentation syntax and not Ruby shell backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed content is Markdown documentation and contains no Ruby source context. The flagged text is displayed as code examples or parameter names.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Alert
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert points to the skill description line. No cryptographic algorithm or related implementation is present on that line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 4 is a human-readable Chinese description of the attendance query skill. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak cryptography.

dangerous_patterns.title: Out-of-Package Relative Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill references a parent-directory file with ../lark-shared/SKILL.md. Reviewers should ensure marketplace packaging includes and audits that dependency.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The relative parent path is explicit in the file. The security impact depends on packaging and whether the referenced shared file is trusted.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local External Command Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill workflow requires running lark-cli commands. Users should run it only in environments where lark-cli is trusted and correctly authenticated.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands are visible and specific. They are normal for this skill but still represent external command execution.

content.value_statement: Attendance record lookup can be slow when users must remember Lark API parameters. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through the correct lark-cli attendance query workflow.
actual_capabilities: Queries personal Lark attendance punch records through lark-cli.
Guides use of the attendance user_tasks query resource.
Requires schema inspection before building command parameters.
Automatically keeps employee_type set to employee_no.
Automatically keeps user_ids as an empty array for personal lookup.
Documents the required attendance:task:readonly permission scope.
limitations: Requires lark-cli to be installed and authenticated.
Only documents the user_tasks.query attendance operation.
Does not edit, approve, or correct attendance records.
Depends on Lark permissions granted to the current user.

use_cases.title: Check Personal Punch Records
use_cases.description: Look up recent attendance punch records without manually building Lark API parameters.

use_cases.title: Support Attendance Questions
use_cases.description: Confirm whether expected punch records exist before contacting HR or a manager.

use_cases.title: Validate CLI Attendance Access
use_cases.description: Test that lark-cli authentication and the attendance read scope can return personal attendance data.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Attendance
prompt_templates.prompt: Use lark-attendance to check my recent Lark attendance punch records.

prompt_templates.title: Query a Date Range
prompt_templates.prompt: Use lark-attendance to query my attendance records for a specific date range. Inspect the schema before choosing parameters.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize Missing Punches
prompt_templates.prompt: Use lark-attendance to retrieve my attendance records, then summarize any days that appear to have missing punch data.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Attendance CLI Access
prompt_templates.prompt: Use lark-attendance to inspect the schema and verify whether my lark-cli setup can query attendance user_tasks records.

output_examples.input: Check my attendance records for this week.
output_examples.output: A concise list of available punch records by date, with start and end times when returned by Lark.

output_examples.input: Find any missing attendance punches last month.
output_examples.output: A summary of dates with incomplete returned punch data, plus a note when no records are available.

output_examples.input: Verify that attendance access works in lark-cli.
output_examples.output: A short status report showing whether schema inspection and the attendance query can run with current permissions.

best_practices: Inspect the attendance schema before building command parameters.
Confirm lark-cli authentication before asking for attendance records.
Use narrow date ranges when reviewing possible missing punches.
anti_patterns: Do not guess Lark API field names without checking the schema.
Do not request attendance data for other users with this personal lookup skill.
Do not use this skill to modify or approve attendance records.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides lark-cli queries for personal Lark attendance punch records.

faq.question: Does it need Lark authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. lark-cli must be installed, authenticated, and granted the required attendance read scope.

faq.question: Can it query other employees?
faq.answer: The skill is designed for personal attendance lookup and keeps user_ids empty.

faq.question: Can it fix missing punches?
faq.answer: No. It only helps query records and summarize returned attendance data.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The skill uses local lark-cli commands and references a shared file outside its folder.
